article_title: EPFL Shrinks a Tabletop-Grade Ultrafast Laser Onto a Chip, Cracking a 20-Year 'Holy Grail' of Integrated Photonics
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: EPFL Shrinks a Tabletop-Grade Ultrafast Laser Onto a Chip, Cracking a 20-Year 'Holy Grail' of Integrated Photonics
content_preview.summary: A Mamyshev oscillator on an erbium-doped silicon nitride chip delivers 1.05 nJ pulses as short as 147 fs, matching tabletop femtosecond lasers.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A team at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL) has built the first integrated ultrafast laser capable of matching the performance of traditional tabletop femtosecond lasers, according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/06/260604044240.htm). Writing in *Nature*, the researchers report a chip-scale device that delivers 1.05 nanojoules in pulses as short as 147 femtoseconds, as described by [EPFL](https://actu.epfl.ch/news/a-ultrafast-lase...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong Analysis piece. Clear structure (Overview, What We Know, Why It Matters, What We Don't Know, Analysis), appropriate technical depth for an integrated-photonics result, 691 words within the Analysis range. Every body paragraph attributes its claims to a named source via inline links.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 source snapshots read from disk. source-0.html.gz (EPFL actu.epfl.ch, 200): CONFIRMS 1.05 nJ / 147 fs, Mamyshev oscillator with nonlinear waveguide between two optical filters, Tobias J. Kippenberg leading the team, Zheru Qiu as 'co-leading author', the verbatim Qiu quote 'This design is especially attractive...erbium-doped silicon nitride chip', the verbatim Kippenberg 'holy grail' quote, 42-cm cavity folded to a match head, more than 1000 cavities per wafer, Nature 03 June 2026 (DOI 10.1038/s41586-026-10517-4), and applications (pollutants, hidden defects, medical diagnostics, optical atomic clocks). EPFL renders the kilowatt quote as 'kilowatt-level peak powers' (plural). source-1.html.gz (ScienceDaily, 200): CONFIRMS tabletop femtosecond comparison, 1.05 nJ / 147 fs, holy grail quote, Mamyshev 'received relatively little attention in integrated photonics', 42 cm cavity / match-head area, and the '20-year holy grail' framing (Kippenberg quote plus the outlet's own 'After 20 years' summary) — the framing is sourced, not editorializing. source-2.html.gz (Phys.org, archive_fallback=true, 200): CONFIRMS the article's verbatim Qiu kilowatt quote 'With kilowatt-level peak power, the chip can drive demanding applications that have long depended on large, expensive laboratory lasers' (singular 'power', matching the article exactly), the Qiu design quote, the Kippenberg holy grail quote, 1.05 nJ / 147 fs, 'first integrated ultrafast laser to rival tabletop femtosecond lasers', and the 'June 3, 2026' publication date that the article cites to Phys.org. source-3.html.gz (optics.org, 200 but 951 bytes): the captured snapshot is an Incapsula bot-challenge page with NO article content. I fell back to a live WebFetch of the optics.org URL, which CONFIRMS all three claims attributed to optics.org: 'erbium-doped silicon nitride' chip, 'more than 1000 laser cavities could be produced at once' at wafer scale, and wafer-scale fabrication. These same claims are independently corroborated by the EPFL and Phys.org snapshots.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every quote is verbatim and correctly attributed: two quotes to Zheru Qiu and one to Tobias J. Kippenberg, exactly as in the snapshots. Every technical specific (1.05 nJ, 147 fs, 42 cm, match-head footprint, >1000 cavities/wafer, kilowatt-level peak power, Nature, 3 June 2026, Mamyshev oscillator, erbium-doped silicon nitride) traces to a fetchable snapshot. Rule 9 check PASSES: no paper-only specific is cited to the paywalled Nature paper; the kilowatt peak power is correctly cited to Phys.org, which carries it verbatim, and Nature is referenced only as the publication venue / for figures that also appear in the open press materials.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and measured throughout. The 'holy grail' language is presented as a sourced characterization (Kippenberg's quote and the outlets' framing), not as the author's own hype. The 'What We Don't Know' section appropriately flags that this is a lab demonstration with no stated yield, stability, or commercialization timeline.
editor_notes.originality: Original topic. No existing article covers this EPFL ultrafast-laser-on-chip result. The nearest neighbor (08-monash-builds-first-single-chip-system...) is a distinct valley-photonics device, not an ultrafast laser; no overlap in sources, facts, or angle.
concerns: The optics.org source snapshot (source-3.html.gz) captured an Incapsula bot-block page rather than the article body, so its three attributed claims were verified against the live optics.org page via WebFetch and corroborated by the EPFL and Phys.org snapshots. Filing this transparently as a clarification in the corrections record.

Two source domains (actu.epfl.ch, optics.org) are not literal entries in config/source_allowlist.txt, though epfl.ch and news.epfl.ch are trusted and both outlets are reputable (EPFL's official news portal and SPIE/IOP's optics.org). Neither is blocklisted; no content concern.
recommendations: Maintainer may wish to add actu.epfl.ch and optics.org to config/source_allowlist.txt (with the accompanying version bump and changelog entry) to suppress this warning on future EPFL/optics submissions.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively clean, fully verified Analysis piece. All quotes, numbers, names, and dates trace verbatim to fetchable snapshots; Rule 9 holds; the holy-grail framing is sourced. The only issues are provenance hygiene (a bot-blocked optics.org snapshot, resolved by live verification, and two reputable-but-unlisted domains). APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS, with a single clarification documenting the optics.org snapshot caveat for the public provenance trail.
